content.text: CODEOWNERS for Prompt Registries and Tools
In GenAI applications, certain file categories carry elevated risk: a one-character typo in a system prompt, a missing
field in a tool schema, or a weakened eval dataset can silently degrade production behaviour without triggering any unit-test failure.
is the GitHub mechanism that mandates review from designated owners before any PR touching those paths can be merged — it is your first line of defence against unreviewed AI artefact changes.

This sub-subtopic covers how to structure CODEOWNERS for the distinct ownership domains that appear in a GenAI repository: prompt registries, tool schemas, agent configurations, evaluation datasets, and infrastructure.

attrs.alt: CODEOWNERS ownership map for a GenAI repository

content.text: Why GenAI Repos Need Purpose-Specific Ownership
Standard codebases assign code owners by functional team — backend, frontend, platform. GenAI repos have an extra layer of concern: the
(prompts, tool schemas, agent configs) are executable specifications consumed by a model at runtime, not compiled by a type-checker. Changes to them:
— prompt text is just a string; it compiles fine whether or not it causes the model to hallucinate.
Do not appear in type errors
— a missing field in a JSON tool schema silently causes the model to omit the tool.
— the model might behave 30% worse on edge cases; no assertion fires.
Ownership rules compensate by requiring a human who understands model behaviour to review every change before it merges.
Anatomy of a GenAI CODEOWNERS File
in the repository root. GitHub evaluates rules top-to-bottom; the last matching pattern wins per file.

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# ===================
# GenAI repository ownership map
# Last-match-wins: more specific patterns below override broad ones.

# --- Default fallback (all files not matched below) ---
*                               @backend-eng

# --- Prompt registry ---
# Any file under prompts/ requires sign-off from the prompt engineering team.
# The NLP lead acts as the tie-breaker reviewer.
prompts/                        @ai-prompt-team @nlp-lead

# System prompts are highest risk; require an additional safety reviewer.
prompts/system/                 @ai-prompt-team @ai-safety-team @nlp-lead

# --- Tool schemas ---
# Tool schemas define the capabilities exposed to the model.
# Security team reviews for prompt-injection surface and capability creep.
tools/                          @ai-tools-team @security-review
tools/schemas/                  @ai-tools-team @security-review @architecture-lead

# --- Agent configurations ---
# Orchestration logic and agent configs affect reasoning loops.
agents/                         @ai-platform-team @architecture-lead
agents/configs/                 @ai-platform-team @architecture-lead
agents/orchestration/           @ai-platform-team @architecture-lead @nlp-lead

# --- Eval datasets and benchmarks ---
# Weakening evals is a silent quality regression.
# Safety team must approve any change to the test oracle.
evals/                          @ai-safety-team @ml-eng
evals/datasets/                 @ai-safety-team @ml-eng @nlp-lead
evals/benchmarks/               @ai-safety-team @ml-eng

# --- Infrastructure and deployment ---
infra/                          @devops @backend-eng
infra/terraform/                @devops @architecture-lead

# --- Application source code ---
src/                            @backend-eng

# --- This file itself requires platform lead sign-off ---
.github/CODEOWNERS              @ai-platform-team @architecture-lead
Enforcement: Requiring Reviews via Branch Protection
CODEOWNERS entries do nothing unless branch protection on
is configured to require code owner review. Set these in your GitHub repository settings:
— activates CODEOWNERS; without this the file is advisory only.
— new commits invalidate prior approvals, preventing stale sign-offs.
— prevents repo admins from bypassing the policy.
Over time, new paths get added without corresponding CODEOWNERS entries — especially as teams create new
subdirectories. Run this audit script in CI to detect coverage gaps:
CI Integration: Running the Audit on Every PR

# .github/workflows/codeowners-audit.yml
name: CODEOWNERS Coverage Audit

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - ".github/CODEOWNERS"
      - "prompts/**"
      - "tools/**"
      - "agents/**"
      - "evals/**"

jobs:
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Check CODEOWNERS coverage
        run: python3 scripts/audit_codeowners_coverage.py

      - name: Verify no unowned files in GenAI dirs
        run: |
          # GitHub CLI can query which files in a PR lack a code owner.
          # Fail if any GenAI path is unowned.
          gh api repos/${{ github.repository }}/contents/.github/CODEOWNERS \
            --jq '.content' | base64 -d | grep -E '^(prompts|tools|agents|evals)' \
            || (echo 'Missing GenAI CODEOWNERS entries' && exit 1)
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
Prompt registries often evolve to version their prompts —
. Each version directory should inherit ownership from the parent but allow the versioning team to self-approve non-breaking additions:

# Version-specific overrides
# New versions still require @nlp-lead, but the owning prompt team
# for that version can self-approve structural changes (not content changes).
prompts/v1/                     @ai-prompt-team-v1 @nlp-lead
prompts/v2/                     @ai-prompt-team-v2 @nlp-lead

# Content files within a version always need safety review too
prompts/v2/system/              @ai-prompt-team-v2 @ai-safety-team @nlp-lead

# Shared templates used across versions are highest-criticality
prompts/templates/              @ai-prompt-team @nlp-lead @ai-safety-team
(directory layout, file naming) can be approved by the team lead alone.
Survives individual turnover; any team member can review
Risk of 'rubber stamp' if team is large
Team can approve, lead is backup/escalation

Recommended pattern: use a small team alias (3–5 people) as the primary owner so any team member can unblock a PR, with one or two named individuals as mandatory additional reviewers for the highest-risk paths (
— the entire value of the mechanism is lost; any developer can merge AI artefact changes without domain review.
— one person on holiday means their PRs block indefinitely.
Over-broad catch-all with no specialised entries
— anyone can remove ownership rules from the file that enforces ownership.
— admins bypass CODEOWNERS if this flag is false, creating an unintentional escape hatch.
with no owner listed disables review for that path, not just broadens it.
Monorepo Considerations for Multi-Service GenAI Repos
When a single repository houses multiple GenAI services, CODEOWNERS paths must distinguish service boundaries. Flat patterns like
grant the same reviewers over every service's prompts — which is only appropriate if a single prompt team owns all of them.

# Multi-service monorepo CODEOWNERS structure

# --- Customer Support Agent ---
services/support-agent/prompts/     @support-prompt-team @nlp-lead
services/support-agent/tools/       @support-tools-team @security-review
services/support-agent/evals/       @ai-safety-team @support-ml-eng

# --- Code Review Bot ---
services/code-review-bot/prompts/   @devtools-prompt-team @nlp-lead
services/code-review-bot/tools/     @devtools-tools-team @security-review
services/code-review-bot/evals/     @ai-safety-team @devtools-ml-eng

# --- Shared prompt templates (cross-cutting; broader sign-off) ---
shared/prompts/                     @ai-prompt-team @nlp-lead @ai-safety-team
shared/tools/                       @ai-tools-team @security-review @architecture-lead
each service should be able to ship independently
. Its AI artefacts should have an owner who understands that service's domain — not a single global prompt team that becomes a cross-service bottleneck.

CODEOWNERS files rot over time: team members leave, team slugs are renamed, and new paths are added without entries. An automated freshness check prevents the file from becoming a fiction nobody enforces.
Run this as a monthly scheduled CI job rather than on every PR — team membership changes infrequently but the GitHub API call is expensive. Alert maintainers when it fails.
Introducing CODEOWNERS to a repository with many unreviewed paths causes immediate friction — every open PR suddenly needs a new sign-off. The
avoids the big-bang: start with the highest-risk paths only, then expand coverage incrementally.

During each phase, measure PR cycle-time impact. If median review wait exceeds one business day for a path, either add more owners to that team or reassess whether the review requirement is appropriate for that path.
A well-structured CODEOWNERS file turns GitHub's merge gate into a

for GenAI repositories. The critical insight is that prompts, tool schemas, eval datasets, and agent configs each carry their own risk profile and require distinct expert reviewers — not the generic backend or platform team that owns application code.
Apply the pattern: define separate ownership blocks for each AI artefact category, require code-owner reviews via branch protection with
, protect the CODEOWNERS file itself, and run an automated coverage audit in CI to catch new paths that slip through without an entry.
